// SLIDE 01 — HOOK

CHAPTER 5 — THE FIVE SUPERVISORY CAPACITIES

This chapter gives a working overview of The Five Supervisory Capacities, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before mo

Chapter 5 — The Five Supervisory Capacit — These are the five things you do that Claude cannot. Name them. Exercise them explicitly. Never delegate them.
NARRATION

This chapter gives a working overview of The Five Supervisory Capacities, focusing on the ideas a reader needs before moving to the next chapter. The chapter moves through Learning outcomes, Opening, The five capacities, PA , Plausibility Auditing, and related ideas. Read it for the main argument, the vocabulary it introduces, and the practical judgment it asks you to develop. These are the five things you do that Claude cannot. Name them. Exercise them explicitly. Never delegate them.

// SLIDE 02 — OPENING

OPENING

Seth was mid-build on the grading tool. He had asked Claude to draft feedback for a stack of ten essay submissions. The

Opening — The drafts were fluent. Each one identified the essay's thesis, named one or two specific strengths, noted one or two ar
NARRATION

Seth was mid-build on the grading tool. He had asked Claude to draft feedback for a stack of ten essay submissions. The CLAUDE.md was in place. The specification was well-formed. Claude returned ten short feedback drafts. The drafts were fluent. Each one identified the essay's thesis, named one or two specific strengths, noted one or two areas for revision. The tone was even. The grammar was clean. Seth scanned them, ready to ship.

// SLIDE 03 — THE FIVE CAPACITIES

THE FIVE CAPACITIES.

·Feedback that describes the essay structure correctly but in language that does not match what you would write to this s
A draft that praises a strength the student has been working on for a monthand the praise is generic enough that the student will not feel seen.
·Output that respects the rubric in form (touches each criterion) and not in intent (treats each criterion as equal weigh
NARRATION

Each capacity is one operation. Each is something Claude Code cannot perform reliably because the operation requires context, judgment, or accountability the model does not have. Hearing the wrong note in Claude's output even when the output passes every surface check.

// SLIDE 04 — HOW THE CAPACITIES FIRE ACROSS

HOW THE CAPACITIES FIRE ACROSS ONE GRADING-TOOL BUILD.

Open Claude Code in the grading project. Run /init if you have not already. Read the CLAUDE.md. choicethis is a CLAUDE.md project, not a Skill project; the rubric will live in CLAUDE.md because every grading session uses i
Compose the specification for "draft feedback for one student." Single submission. Single rubric. Five elements. for thefeedback per submission, not pattern across submissions, on this assignment because the assignment is short and the per-
Run in plan mode, edit via Ctrl+G, approve. Claude executes. Returns the draft. on the draftdoes the feedback match what I would say to this student? on the tone: does the encouragement land for this student who
The draft is close. Two of three rubric criteria are addressed; the third is dropped. againthe dropped criterion is the one the student needs feedback on most. reminds: the spec said all three criteria must be a
/rewind. Restore to before the failed step.revert-and-respecify, not forward-correction.
NARRATION

Trace a grading-tool build with capacities labeled. Open Claude Code in the grading project. Run /init if you have not already. Read the CLAUDE.md. choice: this is a CLAUDE.md project, not a Skill project; the rubric will live in CLAUDE.md because every grading session uses it.

// SLIDE 05 — DIAGNOSING A FAILED BUILD BY A

DIAGNOSING A FAILED BUILD BY ABSENT CAPACITY.

·PF? The spec was "generate feedback for each submission." No mention of avoiding cross-submission pattern leakage. Vague
·PA? Each draft was read once before sending. The pattern leakage was not obvious in a fast scan. PA fired weakly because
·TO? The tool was a single Claude prompt that processed all 23 submissions in one session. The context window saturated;
·IJ? The teacher knew the students well enough that, on a slower read, the wrong-essay feedback would have been caught. T
·EI? The spec from three weeks earlier said each draft is independent. The current execution violated this. EI did not fi
NARRATION

A teacher's grading tool sent 23 feedback drafts to students last week. Eight of them contained the wrong essay's analysis (pattern-completion from a prior draft, like the Seth opening). The teacher discovered the error when a student replied confused. Decompose by capacity. Find the absent one.

// SLIDE 06 — TEACHER BUILD: PREPARE THE GRA

TEACHER BUILD: PREPARE THE GRADING-TOOL BUILD

·PF prep. Write the one-sentence problem formulation for your grading tool. What does it do? Whose work does it touch? Wh
TO plan. For each step of the grading tool you envision, pre-decideCLAUDE.md? Skill (Ch 6)? Hook (Ch 7)? Subagent (Ch 9)? Single prompt? You will revise as Chapters 6–9 introduce each aff
PA training. Identify the two most likely wrong-note moments in your grading toolthe points in the build where Claude's output is most likely to look fluent and be pedagogically wrong. Write the handof
·IJ scaffolding. Identify what you, the teacher, will need to add to every draft that Claude cannot. Per-student knowledg
·EI guardrails. Write the build's three guiding never-rules. Never generate a final grade. Never modify the source submis
NARRATION

You will build a grading tool across Chapters 6, 9. This chapter's build is preparation: design the capacity-exercise points before building. PF prep. Write the one-sentence problem formulation for your grading tool. What does it do? Whose work does it touch? What does it never do? Three sentences. If you cannot answer in three sentences, the formulation is not done.

// SLIDE 07 — CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: CAPACITY-L

CLASSROOM ACTIVITY: CAPACITY-LABELING ON A TRANSCRIPT

Provide a transcript of a Claude Code session for a small build (a data-processing script, a small simulation, a feature

Classroom activity: capacity-labeling on — Their task: label every moment where a supervisory capacity fired. Use the abbreviations PA, PF, TO, IJ, EI. Multiple la
NARRATION

Provide a transcript of a Claude Code session for a small build (a data-processing script, a small simulation, a feature addition). Each prompt and response is unlabeled. Their task: label every moment where a supervisory capacity fired. Use the abbreviations PA, PF, TO, IJ, EI. Multiple labels per moment are allowed. Some moments may have no labels (mechanical execution).

// SLIDE 08 — COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

COMMON MISCONCEPTIONS

"Five things on every prompt."→"Conscious performance every time."→"PA = paranoia."→"Capacities are soft skills."→"The model will improve and replace these."
NARRATION

"Five things on every prompt." No. Each capacity fires at a different moment. A given prompt typically exercises one or two; the full set is exercised across a build. "Conscious performance every time." With practice the capacities fuse into a single conducting motion. The labels are scaffolding; once reflexive, the labels recede.

// SLIDE 09 — EXERCISES

EXERCISES

·(Apply) Take a transcript of your most recent Claude Code session (5+ prompts). Label every capacity-firing moment using
·(Apply) For the grading tool you will build in Chapter 6 onward, write the five-step preparation in the Teacher build se
·(Evaluate) Recall a build that went wrong (yours or a colleague's). Decompose by capacity. Name which was absent. Write
NARRATION

(Apply) Take a transcript of your most recent Claude Code session (5+ prompts). Label every capacity-firing moment using PA, PF, TO, IJ, EI. Identify the capacity you exercised most; the one you exercised least. (Apply) For the grading tool you will build in Chapter 6 onward, write the five-step preparation in the Teacher build section. Save as prep.md alongside CLAUDE.md.

// SLIDE 10 — AI WAYBACK MACHINE

AI WAYBACK MACHINE.

Norbert Wiener (1894–1964) — American mathematician and founder of cybernetics, whose The Human Use of Human Beings (195

AI Wayback Machine — The five supervisory capacities are Wiener's feedback loop made specific to Claude Code. PA catches the drift in Claude'
NARRATION

Norbert Wiener (1894, 1964) , American mathematician and founder of cybernetics, whose The Human Use of Human Beings (1950, rev. 1954) argued that the question for any human-machine system is not whether the machine works, but whether the human-machine feedback loop maintains its goal. Wiener's framing was the loop, not either party. The system's intelligence lives in the corrections the human makes to the machine and the corrections the machine makes possible for the human. Without the corrections, the loop is open and the system drifts.
